---
title: "12.2 Julia and Rust: arrays, ownership, and mutation"
---

## Explanation

After reading [Learn Rust](https://rust-lang.org/learn/), compare Rust with
Julia through a small numerical example. The important difference is not only
syntax. Rust asks you to say, in the function signature, whether a function
only reads data or mutates it.

In Julia, arrays are mutable objects. If two variables refer to the same array,
mutating through one name also changes what the other name sees:

```julia
x = [1.0, 2.0, 3.0]
y = x
y[1] = 10.0

x
```

This is not always bad. It is often convenient. But in a larger program, hidden
mutation through aliases can make bugs hard to locate.

Julia marks mutating functions by convention, often with `!`:

```julia
function scale!(xs, a)
    for i in eachindex(xs)
        xs[i] *= a
    end
    return xs
end
```

The `!` is a useful human convention, but it is not a compiler rule. Rust puts
the distinction into the type of the function argument:

```rust
fn sum(xs: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    xs.iter().sum()
}

fn scale_inplace(xs: &mut [f64], a: f64) {
    for x in xs.iter_mut() {
        *x *= a;
    }
}
```

Here `&[f64]` means a read-only slice, and `&mut [f64]` means a mutable slice.
The function signature tells the reader and the compiler whether the function
can modify the input.

Do not say that Rust forbids all aliasing. Rust allows many immutable aliases:
several parts of the program can read the same data. What safe Rust prevents is
uncontrolled mutable aliasing. In safe Rust, you can have many immutable
references or one mutable reference, but not arbitrary mutation through several
aliases at the same time.

For first examples, use `Vec<f64>` and slices. For multidimensional arrays,
Rust projects often use a crate such as `ndarray`, but the same basic question
remains: which function owns the data, which function only reads it, and which
function mutates it?

## Things to look up

- Rust slice
- `Vec`
- Borrowing
- Mutable reference
- Aliasing

## Exercise

Ask an AI agent to compare the Julia function `scale!(xs, a)` with the Rust
function `scale_inplace(xs: &mut [f64], a: f64)`.

In your answer, explain:

- which version can mutate the input,
- where that fact appears in the code,
- what `&[f64]` means,
- what `&mut [f64]` means,
- why safe Rust prevents uncontrolled mutable aliasing.

Then ask the agent for one short example where Julia aliasing changes an array
through two different names. Explain why the example is convenient in a small
script but risky in a large program.

## Notes for the exercise

- Do not write that Rust arrays are always better than Julia arrays.
- Do not write that all aliasing is impossible in Rust.
- Focus on function signatures and mutation, not on advanced Rust syntax.
- Treat `ndarray` as a later tool for multidimensional arrays, not as the first
  concept to learn.
